reasoning:
The historical base rate for ACLED "Violence against civilians" events in Austria is exceptionally low, averaging around 1 event per year. For the condition to trigger, there must be >10.83 events (effectively 11 or more events) within the specific 30-day windows prior to each resolution date. This represents a more than 100-fold increase over the historical baseline, which would require an unprecedented wave of terrorism, an active insurgency, or severe systematic state repression against non-combatants. 

Currently, as of early August 2025, there is absolutely no evidence of organized armed group conflicts, waves of terrorism, or extreme state clampdowns occurring in Austria. Given that the first resolution date is August 10, 2025, the vast majority of its 30-day window has already passed peacefully, effectively guaranteeing a NO resolution for the first date. For subsequent dates, while it is theoretically possible for an unforeseen crisis to emerge, the base rate of a stable European democracy experiencing 11 distinct armed group attacks on civilians in a single month is negligible. I have assigned probabilities near the 5% minimum bound, increasing slightly for more distant dates to account for general uncertainty over time.
